coredata.dc:title: Activated cascade effect for dual-mode ratiometric and smartphone-assisted visual detection of curcumin and F− based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Siyuan
Wang, Yiru
Guo, Guoqiang
Li, Tingting
Xing, Haoming
Hu, Houwen
Leng, Xuan
Gu, Chenjie
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: The growing persistence of harmful ion or drug molecular residues has always been considered as a matter of concern due to its importance in biological and environmental processes, which requires taking measures to maintain environmental health sustainably and effectively. Inspired by the multi-system and visual quantitative detection of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), we develop a novel cascade nano-system based on dual emission carbon dots for on-site visual quantitative detection of curcumin and fluoride ion (F−). Herein, tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) and m-dihydroxybenzene (m-DHB) are elected as reaction precursors to synthesize dual-emission N-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained N-CDs exhibit dual emission peaks at 426 nm (blue) and 528 nm (green) with quantum yields of 53 % and 71 %, respectively. Then, trace curcumin and F− intelligent off-on-off sensing probe is formed by taking advantage of the activated cascade effect. As for the occurrence of inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the green fluorescence of N-CDs quenches remarkably, called as OFF initial state. Then the curcumin-F− complex leads to the hypochromatic shift of the absorption band from 532 to 430 nm, which activates the green fluorescence of N-CDs, named as ON state. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of N-CDs is quenched due to the FRET, called as OFF terminal state. This system shows good linear relationships from 0 to 35 μM and 0 to 40 μM with low detection limits of 29 nM and 42 nM for curcumin and F− ratiometric detection, respectively. Moreover, a smartphone-assisted analyzer is developed for on-site quantitative detection. Furthermore, we design a logic gate for logistics information storage, which proves the possibility of a logic gate based on N-CDs in practical application. Thus, our work will provide an effective strategy for environmental quantitative monitoring and information storage encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Curcumin
Dual system sensor
Off-on-off fluorescent sensor
Fluoride ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of curcumin and F− in real samples
Smartphone analyzer for curcumin and F− detection
Cascade detection mechanism of curcumin and F−
Cascade fluorescence detection of curcumin and F−
Smartphone-integrated fluorometric sensing platform for real-time detection
Logic gate design for logistics encryption

K C Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084 ), the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University . The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the synthesis mechanism for the N-CDs. (b) TEM image of N-CDs (Inset: the HR-TEM image). (c) FT-IR spectra of Tris, m-DHB and N-CDs. (d) XPS spectrum of N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s and (g) O 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (Inset: the images of N-CDs solution under daylight, 365 nm and 490 nm excitation). (b) Emission spectra of the N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (370–480 nm). (c) Excitation-emission matrix graph map for N-CDs. (d) 13C NMR spectrum of N-CDs. (e) 1H NMR spectrum of N-CDs. (f) Summary of emission wavelength and quantum yield of CDs obtained in this work and previous reports. Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs under different (g) irradiation time in UV light, (h) storage time in daylight and (i) concentrations of NaCl.

The 3D fluorescence spectra and spectral overlap of the absorption spectrum: (a) N-CDs, (b) N-CDs + curcumin and (c) N-CDs/curcumin + F−. (d) Lifetime decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs + curcumin emissions at 528 nm. (e) Lifetime decay curves of N-CDs + curcumin and N-CDs/curcumin + F− emissions at 426 nm. (f) Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism inside of N-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with gradually increased concentration of curcumin. (b) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F528/F426) and the concentration of curcumin. (c) Linear fitting between F528/F426 and the concentration of curcumin within a wide range from 0 to 35 μM. (d) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/curcumin with gradually increased concentration of F−. (e) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F528/F426) and the concentration of F−. (f) Linear fitting between F528/F426 and the concentration of F− within a wide range from 0 to 40 μM.

(a) Schematic of smartphone-assisted analyzer based on N-CDs to detect curcumin and F−. (b) Output lightness values and color scale card with various concentrations of curcumin and F−. (c) Linear plot of LB/LG as a function of curcumin concentration. (d) Linear plot of LB/LG as a function of F− concentration.
(a) The logic gate scheme. (b) The corresponding truth table. (c-d) Flowchart: a hypothetical multi-drop transportation route.
Schematic illustration of N-CDs for the synthesis route and the application in curcumin/F− sensing and smartphone on-site detection.
Determination of curcumin and F− in real samples by using fluorescence and HPLC techniques.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The critical t-value at the 95 % confidence level is 2.78.

head.ce:title: Activated cascade effect for dual-mode ratiometric and smartphone-assisted visual detection of curcumin and F− based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG)
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing persistence of harmful ion or drug molecular residues has always been considered as a matter of concern due to its importance in biological and environmental processes, which requires taking measures to maintain environmental health sustainably and effectively. Inspired by the multi-system and visual quantitative detection of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), we develop a novel cascade nano-system based on dual emission carbon dots for on-site visual quantitative detection of curcumin and fluoride ion (F−). Herein, tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) and m-dihydroxybenzene (m-DHB) are elected as reaction precursors to synthesize dual-emission N-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained N-CDs exhibit dual emission peaks at 426 nm (blue) and 528 nm (green) with quantum yields of 53 % and 71 %, respectively. Then, trace curcumin and F− intelligent off-on-off sensing probe is formed by taking advantage of the activated cascade effect. As for the occurrence of inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the green fluorescence of N-CDs quenches remarkably, called as OFF initial state. Then the curcumin-F− complex leads to the hypochromatic shift of the absorption band from 532 to 430 nm, which activates the green fluorescence of N-CDs, named as ON state. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of N-CDs is quenched due to the FRET, called as OFF terminal state. This system shows good linear relationships from 0 to 35 μM and 0 to 40 μM with low detection limits of 29 nM and 42 nM for curcumin and F− ratiometric detection, respectively. Moreover, a smartphone-assisted analyzer is developed for on-site quantitative detection. Furthermore, we design a logic gate for logistics information storage, which proves the possibility of a logic gate based on N-CDs in practical application. Thus, our work will provide an effective strategy for environmental quantitative monitoring and information storage encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The enrichment of ions has seriously endangered the ecosystem and food safety, which has gradually become a critical issue for public health. Fluoride ion (F−), as an indispensable element for humans and animals, plays an important role in biological, medical and environmental processes (Feng et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a). As the proper F− is beneficial to dental health and the treatment of osteoporosis, it is often used as an additive in toothpaste and medicine (Feng et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020b). Nevertheless, F− also exists in F− minerals or the industrial processes of aluminum smelting and glass processing, thereby seeping into the supply of drinking and irrigation water (Li et al., 2021a; Sahu et al., 2019; Arooj et al., 2021). Concomitantly, excessive F− will harm aquatic organisms and plants and arouse many health problems to human body, including osteoporosis, dental fluorosis and kidney problems (Sahu et al., 2019; Qin et al., 2021). According to the World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines, the maximum allowable level of F− in drinking water is 1.0 mg L−1 (Yang et al., 2017).

Apart from fluoride ions, some small organic molecules also play an essential role in medicine and biology. Curcumin, a natural flavonoid derived from Curcumin longa L., has been commonly used in coloring agents, herbal supplements and food additives (Liu et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2019). Meanwhile, curcumin has been widely used in pharmacology and clinical medicine due to the advantages of anti-cancer, anti-tumor, antioxidant and lowering blood lipids (Schiborr et al., 2010; Mirzaei et al., 2021; Guo et al., 2022). However, excessive curcumin in human beings will generate the deactivation of DNA, causing the decrease of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) levels and tissue necrosis (Mirzaei et al., 2021). Hence, it is of great significance to develop a facile and accurate approach for the simultaneously quantitative detection of curcumin and F− in the fields of food and environments.

Up to now, various detection methods of curcumin and F− have been developed, such as electrode analysis, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), capillary electrophoresis (CE), colorimetric and fluorescence sensing (Schiborr et al., 2010; Zhou et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2004). Among these techniques, the fluorescence method has been widely utilized in the optical sensing field due to its good selectivity, high sensitivity and superior performance compared to other methods (Li et al., 2021b; Su et al., 2021). Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs) have become popular nanomaterials in fluorescence analysis and detection owing to their superior optical properties and excellent biocompatibility (Shen et al., 2020). Till now, a lot of CDs have been developed for the detection of curcumin or F−. For example, Yan et al. reported N-doped carbon dots for the fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode detection of curcumin (Yan et al., 2021). Li et al. developed ratiometric fluorescent CDs for efficient F− detection (Li et al., 2021a). However, few CDs can meet the requirements for simultaneous detection of curcumin and F−. Hence, developing novel CDs for simultaneously quantitative detection of curcumin and F− is of great interest. Several works have been reported about preparing novel CDs for bio/chemical sensing of two substances (Xu et al., 2021a; Zhou et al., 2022). For example, Xu et al. prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots as an “on-off-on” fluorescence probe for sequential detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (Xu et al., 2021a). Zhou et al. synthesized bright red-emitting P, Br co-doped carbon dots as an “off-on” fluorescent probe for Cu2+ and L-cysteine detection (Zhou et al., 2022). However, these fluorescence analysis methods based on a single peak are susceptible to interference from instruments or experiment and cannot realize the visual discrimination of different substances. Meanwhile, it is reported that curcumin can serve as a chelating agent to form a complex with F−, which provides great impetus to achieve simultaneously quantitative detection of curcumin and F− (Wu et al., 2010). Consequently, it is necessary to construct dual emission carbon dots and develop a novel fluorescence dual-mode sensing probe for simultaneously qualitative analysis of curcumin and F− in environment measurement and food industry.

In recent years, multi-mode smartphones have attracted attention as intelligent analyzers because of their portability and operability compared to other traditional laboratory methods. Based on the smartphone imaging platforms, carbon dots have been reported as effective fluorescent nanoprobes by exploiting the visual fluorescence color changes. Hereby, we design a cascade off-on-off fluorescent sensor with colorful consecutive variations for the quantitative determination of curcumin and F−. The dual-emission nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are synthesized by a hydrothermal method using tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) and m-dihydroxybenzene (m-DHB) as the precursor. The N-CDs exhibit bright blue (426 nm) and green (528 nm) emissions, which can be regarded as an off-on-off sensing probe for curcumin and F− detection at the same time. With the increase of curcumin concentration, the green emission of N-CDs quenches significantly (OFF initial state), and the fluorescence intensity exhibits a linear relationship with curcumin concentrations ranging from 0 to 35 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) calculated to be 29 nM. After the cascade addition of F− to N-CDs/curcumin system, the recovery of green fluorescence (ON state) and the quenching of blue fluorescence (OFF terminal state) are achieved simultaneously. This multiple system reveals an excellent linear relationship with F− concentrations in the range of 0 to 40 μM with the LOD of 42 nM. Concomitantly, a dual-mode platform based on N-CDs and a smartphone-assisted analyzer is developed. By analyzing the lightness values of continuous color changes, the on-site quantitative detection of curcumin and F− is realized, and the LODs are 81 nM and 85 nM, respectively. Besides, we also design a logic gate for information storage encryption by the fluorescent changes of N-CDs. All in all, our work provides a novel cascade off-on-off fluorescent sensor and a multifunction smartphone analyzer, which further pave the way for developing dual-mode detection devices, on-site environmental monitoring and information encryption.

Tris (≥99.8 %), m-DHB (99 %), curcumin (98 %), sodium fluoride (NaF, 99.99 %), acetone (≥99.7 %), ethanol (≥99.8 %), NaCl, K2CO3, MgSO4, CaCl2, BaSO4, ZnCl2, NiCl2, CdCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, AlCl3 and all anionic solutions were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All other chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. All solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm) obtained by a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Billerica, MA).

The morphology, particle size and dispersion of N-CDs were measured by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on Empyrean (PANalytical, Netherlands). The infrared spectra were carried out by a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were recorded with a PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The 13C Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and 1H NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker Avance III 600 spectrometer. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectra were performed on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) and a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were taken on a Horiba FL3-111 spectrometer.

The reaction scheme for the preparation and applications of N-CDs were presented in Scheme 1 . 1 mmol (0.121 g) of Tris and 1 mmol (0.110 g) of m-DHB were mixed with 20 mL of deionized water in a 50 mL beaker. After ultrasonic, the mixed solution was transferred to a 75 mL Teflon-lined autoclave in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. Furthermore, after being cooled naturally to room temperature, the solution was purified by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min), filter (0.22 μm filter membrane) and dialysis procedures (MWCO: 100–500 Da, 48 h). Then the powder of N-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying the aqueous solution.

The photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured using quinine sulfate (QY = 54 %) and rhodamine 6G (QY = 95 %) as the standard and calculated with the following equation:
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ st
I x
A st
A x
I st
η x
η st
2
where Φ, I, η, A represent the quantum yield, integrated fluorescence intensity, the refractive index of the solvent and the absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, the absorbance value of the individual solution was kept below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength. The subscript “st” and “x” refers to the reference and the experimental sample, respectively. For the same solution, η x/η st = 1.

First, 20 mg of N-CDs powder was directly dispersed into 200 mL of glycine-NaOH buffer (50 mM, pH = 9). Different volumes of curcumin acetone standard solution (0–100 μM) were added to the N-CDs solution. After completely mixing at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded for quantitative analysis. For the detection of F−, 2 mL N-CDs solution was first mixed with 2 mL curcumin acetone solution (30 μM), and then different concentrations of F− were added into the N-CDs/curcumin mixture solution. To investigate the selectivity of N-CDs for curcumin and F−, various metal ions and anions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, NO3−, SO4 2−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, PO4 3−) were separately introduced into the solution and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of curcumin and F− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk, wastewater and Yongjiang river water were selected for real sample analysis. The milk was bought from the supermarket near Ningbo university. The wastewater and Yongjiang river water were collected from the laboratory and Yongjiang river (Ningbo, Zhejiang), respectively. The 200 mL of water samples (milk, wastewater and Yongjiang river water) were filtered by a 0.22 μm Millipore filter to remove the solid impurity. Then the samples were spiked with different concentrations of curcumin for curcumin detection. For the detection of F−, 1 mL of the water sample, 1.5 mL of N-CDs solution and 2.00 mL of the curcumin acetone solution were mixed together. After that, different concentrations of NaF were spiked into the samples for later detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone analyzer for curcumin and F− detection
ce:section.ce:para: To satisfy the need for on-site dual-mode off-on-off detection of curcumin and F−, we designed a smartphone-integrated analyzer, which is equipped with two excitation light sources (365 nm and 490 nm), a sample cell and a smartphone holder. Given the interference of sunlight and the environment, the equipment was made of highly transparent acrylic, and the exterior was covered with black paint. Meanwhile, the distance between the sample and the light source was fixed to minimize errors in the detection process. During the curcumin detection process, 2 mL of various concentrations of curcumin and 2 mL of N-CDs solution were mixed into the cuvette. Subsequently, the cuvette was inserted into the sample holder to obtain the fluorescence image under the light excitation. Similarly, 2 mL of different concentrations of F− were added into the N-CDs/curcumin mixed solution for the detection of F−. Then, the fluorescence image was directly analyzed using a color-recognizer software (Color Pro) installed on the smartphone. With simple operations, the lightness of blue (LB) and green (LG) information of photos can be obtained quickly.

Herein, N-CDs are prepared using Tris and m-DHB as precursors and heated for 8 h at 180 °C. The synthesis process of N-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1(a). First, m-DHB can act as a carbon source for carbonization, and Tris can serve as a nitrogen source for doping. Meanwhile, the NH2 groups of Tris are highly active and easily react with m-DHB for dehydration and deamination (Tang et al., 2022). Then, the carbonization and deamination occur between the NH2/OH group of Tris and the OH group of m-DHB in the thermal environment. Thereafter, many N-doped small sp2 conjugated structures with -NH2 and -OH groups at edge sites are formed via continuous dehydration, condensation and polymerization (Li et al., 2020c). At last, these sp2 structures are further carbonized and aromatized to a large rigid sp2 conjugated structure and eventually transformed into N-CDs.

The morphology and particle dimension of N-CDs are verified by TEM images in Fig. 1(b). The N-CDs are well-defined spherical with great uniform dispersion. The high-resolution TEM image (inset in Fig. 1(b)) exhibits that N-CDs have a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm attributed to the (100) facet of graphite (Tang et al., 2022). The size distribution of N-CDs ranges from 1 to 8 nm with an average particle size of 4.7 nm (inset in Fig. 1(b)). In XRD pattern (Fig. S1), N-CDs have a broad peak at 21.3°, which is contributed by the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials (Liu et al., 2015). The compositions of the prepared N-CDs are characterized by FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 1(c) indicate the comparison of Tris (blue line), m-DHB (green line) and N-CDs (orange line). For Tris and m-DHB, the absorption peaks at 3100–3509 cm−1, 2921 cm−1, 1385 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, CH, CC and CO, respectively (Li et al., 2020c; Yang et al., 2023). Meanwhile, Tris displays a characteristic peak at 1045 cm−1, which can be attributed to CN stretching vibration (Cui et al., 2022). Compared with Tris and m-DHB, these absorption peaks are also observed in N-CDs, which suggests that N-CDs retain the original functional groups of raw materials during the synthesis process. Meanwhile, the new and strong peaks located at 1630 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of CN and CO, respectively, suggesting the dehydration condensation of Tris and m-DHB during the formation of N-CDs (Cui et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). Thus, these results exhibit the successful synthesis of N-CDs and abundant groups on the surface of N-CDs. The full XPS spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1(d)) shows three peaks at 285.1 eV, 399.4 eV and 532.3 eV, suggesting that N-CDs are composed of C, N and O elements. The contents of C, N and O are calculated as 67.48 %, 6.35 % and 26.17 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1(e), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s contains three peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.5 eV and 286.2 eV, which is attributed to CC/CC, CN/CO and CN/CO, respectively (Shi et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2020). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(f)) has two peaks at 399.3 eV and 401.5 eV, corresponding to CNC and NH groups, respectively (Xu et al., 2021b). The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 1(g)) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.2 eV (CO) and 532.6 eV (CO) (Li et al., 2021c). The above XPS results indicate abundant functional groups on N-CDs, which is concordant with the result of FT-IR analysis.

The optical properties of N-CDs are clarified by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs (black line) exhibits two apparent absorption peaks at around 275 nm and 345 nm, which are ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of conjugated CO/CO bonds, respectively (Yan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). In the visible region, a notable absorption peak at 485 nm appears, which mainly derives from n-π* transition of the conjugated CN bonds (Kang et al., 2022). In the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, it is found that the emission peak at 426 nm appears under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and another emission peak at 528 nm is displayed at 490 nm excitation. Besides, the excitation peak at 365 nm overlaps well with the absorption band at 345 nm, and the excitation peak at 490 nm corresponds to the absorption peak at 485 nm, which demonstrates that the dual emission of N-CDs probably originates from nitrogen and oxygen-related functional groups (Zhao et al., 2019). The N-CDs aqueous solution is faint yellow under the sunlight and exhibits strong blue and green fluorescence under 365 nm and 490 nm excitation light (inset in Fig. 2(a)), respectively. Likewise, N-CDs exhibit two obvious excitation-independent emissions when the excitation moves from 370 nm to 480 nm (Fig. 2(b)), which is attributable to the relatively uniform size distribution and surface states of N-CDs (Pang et al., 2022). Concomitantly, the two-dimensional mapping of N-CDs (Fig. 2(c)) reveals the two distinct emission peaks at 426 nm and 528 nm under the excitation of 365 nm and 490 nm, respectively. To provide a more detailed demonstration of the luminescent functional groups, the 13C NMR and 1H NMR spectra of N-CDs are characterized. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the 13C NMR spectrum exhibits obvious signals from 100 to 110 ppm corresponding to the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms of N-CDs (Xu et al., 2022). The signal at 159 ppm is indicative of CN/CO structure. Besides, the 1H NMR spectrum for N-CDs is exhibited in Fig. 2(e). There are obvious signals in the range of 7.5–8.0 ppm ascribed to aromatic hydrogen (Xu et al., 2022). Moreover, the other peaks located at 8.4 ppm and 8.7 ppm are attributed to the active hydrogen of HCN and CONH, respectively (Xu et al., 2022; Concellon et al., 2018). Hence, combined with FT-IR (Fig. 1(c)), XPS (Fig. 1(e-g)) and NMR results, it can be found that these oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CDs play an irreplaceable role in the dual fluorescence emission process. Subsequently, the QYs of N-CDs are calculated as 53 % and 71 % for the blue and green emissions, respectively, and are comparable with those of most CDs reported in previous studies (Table S1 and Fig. 2(f)).

In practical sensing, the fluorescence stabilities of N-CDs are significantly important. Hence, the effects of light irradiation (365 nm), long-term storage and ionic strength (based on NaCl concentration) are investigated. As shown in Fig. 2(g-i), the prepared N-CDs show relatively stable fluorescence intensity under continuous UV irradiation for 30 h, long-term storage at room temperature for 50 days and the concentration of NaCl from 0 to 1 M, which suggests excellent optical stability and photobleaching resistance of N-CDs. Thus, N-CDs can serve as a promising fluorescent probe for actual sensing due to their good optical and salt stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cascade detection mechanism of curcumin and F−
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the sensing mechanism of N-CDs to curcumin and F−, we monitor the UV–vis absorption and 3D fluorescence spectra under different conditions. Fig. 3(a) shows the 3D fluorescence spectra of N-CDs. Subsequently, as shown in Fig. 3(b), the absorption spectrum of curcumin shows a broad absorption band at 530 nm, which has a large overlap with the excitation peak at 490 nm and a small overlap with the emission peak at 528 nm of N-CDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of green emission (490 nm, 528 nm) due to the synergistic effect of internal filtering effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2021). Whereafter, with the addition of F−, the absorption band diminishes and behaves a blue-shift to 430 nm owing to the formation of the complex between curcumin and F− (Fig. 3(c)). This transformation vastly reduces the overlap between the two spectra and intercepts the IFE and FRET, leading to the recovery of green fluorescence. Meanwhile, the absorption of curcumin-F− greatly overlaps with the emission peak at 426 nm of N-CDs. Therefore, we reason that the occurrence of FRET between the emission peak at 426 nm and curcumin-F− complex plays a vital role in quenching the blue emission (Liao et al., 2021). To further confirm the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs, N-CDs + curcumin and N-CDs/curcumin + F− are investigated. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the average fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N-CDs/curcumin at 528 nm are 2.75 ns and 2.69 ns, respectively. These nearly identical values confirm that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs is caused by a main factor of IFE and a subordinated factor of FRET (Liao et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019). After the addition of F−, the average lifetimes of N-CDs/curcumin and N-CDs/curcumin + F− at 426 nm are calculated to be 4.07 ns and 3.60 ns (Fig. 3(e)), respectively. The decreased fluorescence lifetime implies the occurrence of the FRET process. In addition, we also explore the Zeta potentials to confirm the FRET mechanism for F− detection. As shown in Fig. S2, the Zeta potentials of N-CDs + curcumin and F− are estimated to be +21.3 mV and −9.5mV, respectively, and the positive charges on the surface of N-CDs + curcumin form an electrostatic attraction with negative charges of F− (Wang et al., 2019). From these results, the schematic diagram of the activated cascade effect for the off-on-off detection mechanism of curcumin and F− is depicted in Fig. 3(f).

To obtain the optimal sensing capacity, critical detection parameters affecting the experiments are optimized. Firstly, the pH and solvent are assessed for curcumin detection. Water is generally used as a good solution for practical sample analysis. However, as curcumin is insoluble in water and easily soluble in acetone, a water/acetone mixed solution with a volume ratio of 1:1 is adopted to dissolve curcumin. The pH value is also a crucial biochemical index in the detection process. When the pH is adjusted to 9, the absorption of curcumin water/acetone mixed solution satisfies the requirements of IFE/FRET-based detection of curcumin (Fig. S3(a)), while the curcumin/F− water/acetone mixed solution satisfies the requirements of FRET-based detection of F− (Fig. S3(b)). Therefore, it is confirmed that pH can be fixed at 9 for detection. Besides, the water/ethanol mixed solution (volume ratio = 1:1) is also explored due to the excellent solubility of curcumin in ethanol. As shown in Fig. S4, as the pH changes from 4 to 11, no apparent changes occur in the absorption spectrum of the mixed solution. Therefore, ethanol cannot act as a suitable experimental solvent in detection. Subsequently, the concentration of curcumin (Fig. S5) and optimal response time (Fig. S6) are also investigated. The water/acetone mixed solution (a volume ratio of 1:1) at pH = 9 and an incubation time of 3 min are chosen as the optimal conditions for curcumin detection. The same water/acetone mixed solution, 30 μM curcumin and 4 min incubation time are chosen as the optimal criteria for F− detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cascade fluorescence detection of curcumin and F−
ce:section.ce:para: The above optimal conditions are set for the cascade detection of curcumin and F−. The N-CDs exhibit dual-emission at 426 nm (blue) and 528 nm (green) under the excitation of 365 nm and 490 nm, respectively. With the increase of curcumin concentration (0–100 μM), the intensity of green emission at 528 nm gradually decreases, while the blue emission peak at 426 nm almost maintains unchanged invariably (Fig. 4(a)). Based on the unique property, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for curcumin detection is established. The relationship between F528/F426 (F426 and F528 are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 426 nm and 528 nm, respectively) and curcumin concentration (0–100 μM) in Fig. 4(b) exhibits an excellent linear correlation (R2 = 0.998) at the curcumin concentration range of 0–35 μM (Fig. 4(c)), and the LOD is calculated to be 29 nM. Subsequently, a fixed proportion of N-CDs/curcumin solution (0.1 mg mL−1: 30 μM) is used to form a F− sensing probe due to the activated cascade effect of curcumin. Upon the addition of F− in the concentration range of 0–120 μM, the intensity of green emission at 528 nm gradually recovers, while the blue emission peak at 426 nm gradually decreases (Fig. 4(d)). As shown in Fig. 4(e), the intensity ratio of F528/F426 increases with the increase of F− concentration from 0 to 120 μM. And a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) is obtained between F528/F426 and F− concentrations (0–40 μM) with the LOD of 42 nM (Fig. 4(f)), which is far below the maximum level of F− (1.0 mg L−1) in drinking water permitted by WHO (Yang et al., 2017). Moreover, the fluorescence images of N-CDs with various concentrations of curcumin and F− under the excitation of 365 nm and 490 nm are shown in Fig. S7. As a result, a dynamic cascade off-on-off system is achieved for the simultaneous detection of curcumin and F−.

To evaluate the selectivity of the N-CDs towards curcumin and N-CDs/curcumin towardsF−, a series of mental ions and anions are added to the solution. As shown in Fig. S8, various interfering substances, including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, NO3−, SO4 2−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2− and PO4 3−, result in little change of the dual-emission fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs and N-CDs/curcumin. Thus, our cascade off-on-off system exhibits outstanding anti-interference ability, which indicates that the system has the potential for on-site qualitative identification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-integrated fluorometric sensing platform for real-time detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the traditional detection process needs expensive and cumbersome instruments to achieve high sensitivity and accuracy. To satisfy the requirements of on-site and rapid quantitative detection, the smartphone-integrated analyzer has attracted great attention due to its simple operation and excellent sensitivity. However, the single-mode detection method limits the development of the multi-emission CDs. Therefore, a handheld smartphone-integrated fluorometric sensing platform is developed to achieve dual-mode off-on-off detection of curcumin and F−. Therewith, the analysis process is achieved by using the software (Color Pro) installed in the smartphone (Fig. 5(a)). As shown in Fig. 5(b), the lightness of blue (LB) and green (LG) of the fluorescence images under two excitation light sources are converted into digital values with the corresponding color scale cards fabricated (each experiment is repeated 3 times). By recording the relationship between the value of LB/LG and the concentration of curcumin, an excellent linear range from 0 to 35 μM is obtained for the curcumin detection (Fig. 5(c)), and the LOD is calculated to be 81 nM. For F− detection, a linear range from 0 to 40 μM with the LOD of 85 nM is shown in Fig. 5(d).

Subsequently, we measure curcumin/F− in real samples (milk, wastewater and Yongjiang river water) to evaluate the practicability of the smartphone-based sensing method. Meanwhile, the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis is selected as a reference method (Hannah, 1986; Korany et al., 2017). As shown in Table 1 , for both fluorescence and HPLC techniques, the recoveries range from 97.3 % to 104.1 % with a relative standard deviation in percentage (%RSD) < 5 %. Moreover, the t-test shows no significant differences between both methods at a 95 % confidence level. These results demonstrate that the smartphone analyzer has great practicability and reliability for on-site detection of curcumin/F− in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic gate design for logistics encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the cascade-sensitive response of N-CDs towards curcumin and F−, a fluorescence logic system with the function of executing a dual-input logic operation is properly designed in Fig. 6(a). In this devised logic system, N-CDs act as logic gates, while curcumin and F− serve as the chemical inputs. For the input, their absence and presence are set as Boolean logic functions of 0 and 1. Correspondingly, the normal fluorescence intensity and quenched fluorescence are set as 1 and 0. Thus, the parameters and results of the cascade N-CDs-based logic operations are exhibited in the truth table (Fig. 6(b)). In the absence of any input (0,0), the two distinct emission peaks at 426 nm and 528 nm show an output of (1, 1). With the addition of F−, the fluorescence intensities at 426 nm and 528 nm still demonstrate strong performance, and the output is kept as (1, 1). When the input is (1, 0), the fluorescence intensity at 528 nm is substantially quenched (output is (1, 0)). Moreover, in the presence of curcumin and F− inputs (1, 1), the emission peak at 528 nm produces fluorescence recovery, and the emission peak at 426 nm exhibits fluorescence quenching (output is (0, 1)). All these results confirm that the cascade N-CDs sensing platform forms the off-on-off switch and can be used to construct the logic gate function.

As logic gate operation combined with quick response (QR) code is a potential digital encryption method, we envision a possible hypothetical logistics transportation route to cargo information encryption storage and classification through the logic gate function (Fig. 6(c)). Firstly, a paper-based microfluidic device is exploited to monitor the signal input. The actual device, preparation method and principle of detection are shown in Fig. S9. 30 μL of N-CDs is preloaded on the test area at both ends of the chip. After the solution dries, the chip will be pasted on the outside of the packages and exhibits blue and green fluorescence under the 365 nm and 490 nm excitation (Fig. S10). When the package arrives at a designated transfer station, different solutions (curcumin, F− or curcumin + F−) will be transferred to the central sample area. Then the solution will enter the test zone through two parallel hydrophilic channels to react with N-CDs (Fig. S11). Subsequently, the changed signs (0 or 1) will be recognized under the 365 nm and 490 nm excitation. As shown in Fig. S12, the appearance of the chip in sunlight has no noticeable change, while the two test areas exhibit different fluorescence changes. Simultaneously, through identifying the specific logic signals (0 or 1), the QR code containing the specific destination and information of the package will be exhibited via the computer (Fig. 6(d)). Only the correct logic can obtain information about the particular package, which will be dropped off at its destination, and the rest of the package will continue to be shipped with a new paper-based device. Finally, through specific logical operations, the information encryption and transportation classification of the packages that are transported to different places are realized. The whole operation process is simplified and presented in Video S1. These results indicate that the logic gate with QR codes based on the cascade N-CDs sensing platform has the potential as a digital encryption tool for the security of logistics transportation.

As logic gate operation combined with quick response (QR) code is a potential digital encryption method, we envision a possible hypothetical logistics transportation route to cargo information encryption storage and classification through the logic gate function (Fig. 6(c)). Firstly, a paper-based microfluidic device is exploited to monitor the signal input. The actual device, preparation method and principle of detection are shown in Fig. S9. 30 μL of N-CDs is preloaded on the test area at both ends of the chip. After the solution dries, the chip will be pasted on the outside of the packages and exhibits blue and green fluorescence under the 365 nm and 490 nm excitation (Fig. S10). When the package arrives at a designated transfer station, different solutions (curcumin, F− or curcumin + F−) will be transferred to the central sample area. Then the solution will enter the test zone through two parallel hydrophilic channels to react with N-CDs (Fig. S11). Subsequently, the changed signs (0 or 1) will be recognized under the 365 nm and 490 nm excitation. As shown in Fig. S12, the appearance of the chip in sunlight has no noticeable change, while the two test areas exhibit different fluorescence changes. Simultaneously, through identifying the specific logic signals (0 or 1), the QR code containing the specific destination and information of the package will be exhibited via the computer (Fig. 6(d)). Only the correct logic can obtain information about the particular package, which will be dropped off at its destination, and the rest of the package will continue to be shipped with a new paper-based device. Finally, through specific logical operations, the information encryption and transportation classification of the packages that are transported to different places are realized. The whole operation process is simplified and presented in Video S1. These results indicate that the logic gate with QR codes based on the cascade N-CDs sensing platform has the potential as a digital encryption tool for the security of logistics transportation.

In summary, N-CDs are prepared by a hydrothermal method with Tris and m-DHB as raw materials and exhibit blue and green emissions with high QYs of 53 % and 71 %. Then a cascade off-on-off fluorescent dual-mode sensor is constructed to monitor the curcumin and F− anion, which presents a detection limit of 29 nM and 42 nM for curcumin and F−, respectively, based on ratiometric detection method. Subsequently, we developed a smartphone-assisted dual-mode platform based on N-CDs to realize on-site quantitative detection, and the LODs are 81 nM and 85 nM through the analysis of lightness values of fluorescence images. Finally, a logic gate design for logistics encryption is demonstrated, which expands the potential of quantitative detection and logic gate in practical application. Overall, this work realizes the activated cascade effect of the N-CDs-based sensing platform for dual-mode visual detection and reliable information encryption.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Video S1
The logic gate design for logistics encryption process.
Video S1
Supplementary material
Image 1

Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: [details of any supplementary information available should be included here]. Figures (Figs. S1-S12) and Video S1. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.162277.

Siyuan Tang analyzed experimental results and wrote the manuscript. Yiru Wang involved in analyzing experimental results. Guoqiang Guo and Tingting Li assisted in analyzing experimental results and software data processing. Haoming Xing and Houwen Hu assisted in investigation and formal analysis. Xuan Leng and Chenjie Gu assisted in reviewing the manuscript. Da Chen conducted the experiments, revised the manuscript and provided Funding acquisition. All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084), the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.
